Information literacy: critical source evaluation, data provenance assessment, and methodological scrutiny.
Digital preservation addresses format obsolescence and media decay via migration and emulation.
The Memex (Bush, 1945) envisioned hypertext links; Berners-Lee realized aspects of this vision.
Misinformation spreads faster than corrections; source reputation and lateral reading are countermeasures.
Open access (Budapest Declaration, 2002) removes paywalls from peer-reviewed research.
Ontologies formally define concepts and relations; used in AI, bioinformatics, and information retrieval.
ETHICS, RIGHTS & JUSTICE SYSTEMS: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) articulates universal civil, political, and social rights.
Rawls's difference principle: inequalities are just only if they benefit the least-advantaged members.
Procedural justice (fair processes) and distributive justice (fair outcomes) both affect legitimacy.
US exoneration rates (~180 death row exonerations since 1973) raise wrongful execution concerns.
US incarceration rate is the highest globally; racial disparities are well-documented.
ICC jurisdiction is complementary; it prosecutes only when national courts are unwilling or unable.
Restorative justice programs reduce reoffending; victim-offender mediation shows higher satisfaction.
UNCRC (1989) 54 articles protect children's survival, protection, participation, and development.
Non-refoulement: states cannot return refugees to territories where they face persecution.
CRPD (2006) recognizes rights to accessibility, independent living, and equal participation.
Corporate ESG frameworks hold firms accountable to environmental, social, and governance standards.
Transitional justice: truth commissions, reparations, and prosecutions address post-conflict atrocities.
Animal sentience grounds moral consideration in utilitarian and rights-based frameworks.
Digital rights: privacy, free expression, and access are contested in surveillance capitalism contexts.
Environmental justice demands fair treatment regardless of race or income in environmental regulation.
SURVIVAL, PRIMAL & ANCESTRAL SKILLS: Fire by friction requires dry tinder, spindle, hearthboard, and sustained pressure generating about 300 degrees C.
Polaris lies within 1 degree of true north; Southern Cross and pointer stars indicate the south celestial pole.
Boiling water for 1 minute kills pathogens; chemical treatment with iodine or chlorine addresses bacteria.
Positive plant identification is essential; many toxic species closely mimic edible ones.
Debris hut construction creates insulating dead-air space and can maintain near-body temperature.
Animal gait patterns and track depth indicate species and speed for tracking.
Atlatl and bow-and-arrow hunting require knowledge of animal behavior and seasonal movement.
Reverse-wrap cordage from plant fiber is essential for tools, shelter, and trapping.
Field dressing prevents meat spoilage; skinning along the midline preserves hide and meat.
Wilderness first aid: direct pressure for bleeding; improvised splints for fractures; prevent hypothermia.
Magnetic declination correction aligns compass readings to true north for accurate orienteering.
Drying, smoking, fermentation, and salting preserve food without refrigeration.
Seasonal foraging calendars track berry, root, fungi, and green availability by climate zone.
Knapping (flint, obsidian, chert) uses percussion and pressure flaking to produce sharp stone edges.
Ancestral land stewardship: controlled burns and polyculture gardens managed landscapes sustainably.
CYBERSECURITY: A zero-day exploit targets a vulnerability unknown to the vendor; no patch exists at time of attack.
STRIDE threat model categorizes threats as Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, and Elevation of privilege.
Post-quantum cryptography (PQC) uses algorithms resistant to quantum computer attacks; NIST standardized CRYSTALS-Kyber and CRYSTALS-Dilithium in 2024.
Kyber key encapsulation mechanism (KEM) uses module learning-with-errors (MLWE) hardness; provides IND-CCA2 security at NIST Level 1, 3, or 5.
Dilithium digital signature scheme uses lattice-based cryptography; Dilithium5 provides security equivalent to 256-bit AES.
A man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack intercepts communications between two parties; TLS certificate pinning and mutual authentication mitigate it.
End-to-end encryption (E2EE) ensures only communicating parties can read messages; Signal Protocol uses Double Ratchet and X3DH.
SQL injection inserts malicious SQL into input fields; parameterized queries and prepared statements prevent it.
Buffer overflow overwrites adjacent memory; stack canaries, ASLR, and DEP/NX mitigate exploitation.
Penetration testing simulates attacker behavior; black-box, white-box, and grey-box testing differ by attacker knowledge.
Ransomware encrypts victim files and demands payment; offline backups and network segmentation are primary defenses.
Social engineering exploits human psychology; phishing, vishing, and pretexting target credentials without technical exploits.
Zero-trust architecture assumes no implicit trust; every request is authenticated, authorized, and encrypted regardless of network location.
Threat intelligence (IOCs, TTPs) feeds detection systems; STIX and TAXII standardize sharing formats.
CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) provides a standardized identifier for publicly known security vulnerabilities.
The OWASP Top 10 lists the most critical web application security risks including injection, broken auth, and IDOR.
Steganography hides data within carrier files; LSB embedding conceals messages in image pixel data.
AES-256 in GCM mode provides both confidentiality and authenticity; it is the current symmetric encryption standard.
PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) uses certificate authorities to bind public keys to identities via X.509 certificates.
Defense in depth layers security controls so that failure of one does not compromise the whole system.
